docs.title: Model Router
docs.text:
Model Router is an intelligent AI model orchestration layer for Claude. It analyses every incoming task, scores its complexity, and automatically routes it to the most cost-effective Claude model — Opus, Sonnet, or Haiku — while tracking token usage and cost in real time.

📷 Screenshot: Model Router dashboard showing live cost stats, model usage chart, and sidebar navigation

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Automatic complexity scoring — five-signal weighted formula picks the right model every time
  • Task decomposition — oversized tasks are split into type-aware subtasks (coding by file, reviews by concern, docs by section) and merged into a single coherent result
  • Prompt optimization — three-pass compression (whitespace → boilerplate stripping → middle-truncation) keeps token counts and costs low
  • In-memory result cache — configurable TTL avoids redundant API calls for repeated prompts
  • Persistent usage tracking — every call is recorded to SQLite; query totals by model, session, or time window
  • Live dashboard — Starlette + SSE web UI for real-time cost monitoring, task history, and route previews
  • Provider-agnostic design — add OpenAI, Gemini, or any other provider with a single file
  • CLI & Python API — use from the terminal or embed in any Python application

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Requirement Version Python ≥ 3.11 claude-agent-sdk latest pydantic ≥ 2.0 PyYAML ≥ 6.0 starlette + uvicorn latest sse-starlette latest httpx latest anyio latest

An Anthropic API key must be available to the claude-agent-sdk (typically via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY).

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# 1. Clone and enter the project
git clone <repo-url>
cd model-router

# 2. Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 3. Set your Anthropic API key
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

# 4. Preview how a prompt would be routed (no API call)
python main.py preview "Refactor this microservice to use async database queries"

# 5. Start the live dashboard
python main.py serve
# → open http://localhost:8765

docs.title: Python API quick start
docs.text:
import anyio
from model_router import ModelRouter, TaskRequest, TaskType

router = ModelRouter()

result = anyio.run(router.route_and_run, TaskRequest(
    prompt="Implement a binary search function in Python",
    task_type=TaskType.CODING,
    quality_requirement=0.7,
))

print(result.result)
print(f"Model : {result.decision.selected_model}")
print(f"Tokens: {result.total_input_tokens + result.total_output_tokens}")
print(f"Cost  : ${result.total_cost_usd:.6f}")

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text:
• Architecture — system components, data flow, and design decisions
  • Configuration — all config options, routing thresholds, and environment variables
  • API Reference — every public class, method, and data structure
  • Guides — step-by-step tutorials

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
Model Router is a thin orchestration layer that sits between your application code (or CLI) and the Claude API. It intercepts every task request, evaluates its complexity, selects the most appropriate — and cost-efficient — model, optionally splits large tasks into parallel subtasks, executes them through a provider adapter, and stores every call in a local SQLite database.

graph TD
    CLI["CLI / Python API<br/>(main.py)"]
    Router["ModelRouter<br/>router.py"]
    Optimizer["TokenOptimizer<br/>optimizer.py"]
    Scorer["ComplexityScorer<br/>scorer.py"]
    Decomposer["TaskDecomposer<br/>decomposer.py"]
    Registry["ModelRegistry<br/>registry.py"]
    Provider["ClaudeProvider<br/>providers/claude.py"]
    SDK["claude-agent-sdk"]
    Tracker["UsageTracker<br/>tracker.py"]
    DB["SQLite<br/>data/usage.db"]
    Dashboard["Dashboard Server<br/>server/app.py + routes.py"]
    Browser["Browser UI<br/>server/static/"]
    Config["config/models.yaml"]

    CLI -->|TaskRequest| Router
    Dashboard -->|TaskRequest| Router
    Browser <-->|REST / SSE| Dashboard

    Router --> Optimizer
    Router --> Scorer
    Scorer --> Registry
    Router --> Decomposer
    Decomposer --> Registry
    Router --> Provider
    Provider --> SDK
    SDK -->|AssistantMessage / ResultMessage| Provider
    Provider -->|ExecutionRecord| Router
    Router --> Tracker
    Tracker --> DB
    Dashboard --> Tracker
    Registry -->|reads| Config

docs.title: ModelRouter — the central orchestrator
docs.text:
ModelRouter (in model_router/router.py) owns the complete request pipeline. It wires all sub-components together and exposes two public coroutines:

  • route_task(request) — analyse and return a RoutingDecision without executing
  • route_and_run(request) — full pipeline: compress → score → decompose? → execute → track → return

docs.title: ComplexityScorer — model selector
docs.text:
ComplexityScorer (scorer.py) converts a raw TaskRequest into a numeric complexity score (0.0–1.0) using five weighted signals:

Signal Weight Notes Estimated token count 35% Normalised to 8 000 tokens Keyword complexity 30% Regex patterns weighted +/− Quality requirement 20% Caller-supplied 0–1 value Architecture signals 10% Detects design-related language Multi-file references 5% Two or more file paths in prompt

The score is compared to two configurable thresholds to select between Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku.

docs.title: TaskDecomposer — large-task splitter
docs.text:
When the estimated token count exceeds decompose_threshold × context_window, TaskDecomposer (decomposer.py) breaks the request into typed subtasks:

Task type Decomposition strategy coding One subtask per referenced file; Sonnet per file, Opus for integration review Four parallel concern passes (security, performance, correctness, style); Opus synthesises docs Three sections (overview, API reference, examples); Opus assembles final document reasoning Two halves analysed by Sonnet; Opus synthesises chat / fallback Generic chunk-based split

docs.title: TokenOptimizer — cost reducer
docs.text:
TokenOptimizer (optimizer.py) operates on two axes:

  1. Prompt compression — three-pass lossy-last strategy applied when estimated tokens exceed compress_threshold:
  2. Pass 1: whitespace normalisation (lossless)
  3. Pass 2: strip numbered examples beyond the 5th (near-lossless)

  4. Pass 3: middle-truncation, preserving the first and last 35% (lossy)

  5. Result caching — SHA-256 keyed in-memory cache (prompt + task type) with configurable TTL. Prevents redundant API calls for identical prompts.

docs.title: ModelRegistry — config loader
docs.text: ModelRegistry (registry.py) parses config/models.yaml and provides typed access to model capabilities, routing config, and token estimation parameters. Supports hot-reload via reload().

docs.title: ClaudeProvider — SDK adapter
docs.text: ClaudeProvider (providers/claude.py) is the only built-in provider. It wraps claude-agent-sdk's async query() function, accumulates token usage from streaming messages, and returns a populated ExecutionRecord.

docs.title: UsageTracker — persistent telemetry
docs.text: UsageTracker (tracker.py) writes every ExecutionRecord to SQLite using asyncio.to_thread so disk I/O never blocks the event loop. It exposes async query methods used by the dashboard API.

docs.title: Dashboard server
docs.text: server/app.py creates a Starlette application that mounts: - REST + SSE JSON API (defined in server/routes.py) - Static files — the single-page dashboard (server/static/)

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Caller
    participant R as ModelRouter
    participant O as TokenOptimizer
    participant S as ComplexityScorer
    participant D as TaskDecomposer
    participant P as ClaudeProvider
    participant T as UsageTracker

    C->>R: route_and_run(TaskRequest)
    R->>O: get_cached(key)
    alt cache hit
        O-->>R: cached result
        R-->>C: RoutedResult (cost=0)
    else cache miss
        R->>O: compress_prompt(prompt)
        O-->>R: optimised prompt
        R->>S: build_routing_decision(request)
        S-->>R: (TaskFeatures, RoutingDecision)
        alt decomposed
            R->>D: decompose(request, features)
            D-->>R: list[Subtask]
            loop each subtask
                R->>P: execute(sub_request, model)
                P-->>R: (text, ExecutionRecord)
            end
            R->>D: combine(results)
        else direct
            R->>P: execute(request, model)
            P-->>R: (text, ExecutionRecord)
        end
        R->>T: record(ExecutionRecord)
        R->>O: set_cached(key, result)
        R-->>C: RoutedResult
    end

docs.title: Why SQLite?
docs.text: All deployments are single-process; SQLite avoids an external dependency while still providing full SQL query capability for the dashboard. The schema includes three indexes (timestamp, model, session_id) to keep dashboard queries fast.

docs.title: Why SSE for the dashboard?
docs.text: Server-Sent Events provide one-way push from the server to the browser without WebSocket complexity. The /api/stats/live endpoint emits updated totals every 2 seconds, giving a near-real-time feel with minimal overhead.

docs.title: Provider abstraction
docs.text: BaseProvider (two abstract methods: execute and supports_model) makes adding a new AI provider a single-file change. No modifications to ModelRouter are needed — just register the new instance in __init__.

docs.title: Fallback chain
docs.text: The scorer assigns a one-tier-down fallback for every selection (Opus → Sonnet → Haiku). The ClaudeProvider receives fallback_model_string and can retry on transient errors without re-scoring.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
All runtime configuration lives in config/models.yaml. The file has three top-level sections: models, routing, and token_estimation.

Changes can be applied without restarting the server — click Reload in the dashboard Settings page or call router.reload_config() programmatically.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Variable Required Description ANTHROPIC_API_KEY Yes API key used by claude-agent-sdk to authenticate with Anthropic

No other environment variables are used by default. Provider keys for future integrations (e.g. OPENAI_API_KEY) would be consumed by the respective provider implementation.

docs.title: models Section
docs.text:
Each entry defines one model available for routing.

Field Type Description id string Internal identifier used in routing logic (e.g. claude-opus-4-6) display_name string Human-readable name shown in the dashboard provider string Provider key — must match a registered BaseProvider (e.g. claude) model_string string Exact string passed to the provider SDK context_tokens integer Maximum context window in tokens cost_input_per_1k float USD cost per 1 000 input tokens cost_output_per_1k float USD cost per 1 000 output tokens capability_rank integer 1 (weakest) to 10 (strongest); used for budget-based selection max_output_tokens integer Maximum tokens the model will generate per response strengths list[string] Informational tags shown in the dashboard

docs.title: Built-in models
docs.text: ID Display name Capability rank Input $/1k Output $/1k claude-opus-4-6 Claude Opus 4.6 10 $0.015 $0.075 claude-sonnet-4-6 Claude Sonnet 4.6 7 $0.003 $0.015 claude-haiku-4-5 Claude Haiku 4.5 3 $0.00025 $0.00125

docs.title: routing Section
docs.text:
Controls model-selection thresholds and pipeline behaviour.

Key Type Default Description opus_threshold float 0.72 Complexity score ≥ this value routes to Opus sonnet_threshold float 0.38 Complexity score ≥ this value routes to Sonnet; below routes to Haiku decompose_threshold float 0.75 Decompose when estimated_tokens > context_tokens × this value compress_threshold int 6000 Compress the prompt when estimated tokens exceed this value cache_ttl_seconds int 300 In-memory result cache TTL in seconds

docs.title: Routing decision logic
docs.text:
score = 0.35×token_score + 0.30×keyword_complexity + 0.20×quality_req
        + 0.10×architecture_signal + 0.05×multifile_signal

if cost_budget set:
    pick highest-capability model whose estimated cost ≤ budget
elif score >= opus_threshold:
    → claude-opus-4-6
elif score >= sonnet_threshold:
    → claude-sonnet-4-6
else:
    → claude-haiku-4-5

docs.title: token_estimation Section
docs.text:
Parameters used to estimate token counts from character counts before making an API call.

Key Type Default Description prose_chars_per_token float 3.8 Characters per token for prose/natural-language content code_chars_per_token float 2.5 Characters per token for code (code is denser) overhead_factor float 1.2 Multiplier to account for system prompt and response preamble overhead

docs.title: Complete config/models.yaml Example
docs.text:
models:
  - id: claude-opus-4-6
    display_name: "Claude Opus 4.6"
    provider: claude
    model_string: "claude-opus-4-6"
    context_tokens: 200000
    cost_input_per_1k: 0.015
    cost_output_per_1k: 0.075
    capability_rank: 10
    max_output_tokens: 32000
    strengths: [reasoning, architecture, coding, review, complex-analysis]

  - id: claude-sonnet-4-6
    display_name: "Claude Sonnet 4.6"
    provider: claude
    model_string: "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    context_tokens: 200000
    cost_input_per_1k: 0.003
    cost_output_per_1k: 0.015
    capability_rank: 7
    max_output_tokens: 16000
    strengths: [coding, docs, review, chat, summarization]

  - id: claude-haiku-4-5
    display_name: "Claude Haiku 4.5"
    provider: claude
    model_string: "claude-haiku-4-5"
    context_tokens: 200000
    cost_input_per_1k: 0.00025
    cost_output_per_1k: 0.00125
    capability_rank: 3
    max_output_tokens: 8000
    strengths: [chat, summarization, simple-coding, classification]

routing:
  opus_threshold: 0.72
  sonnet_threshold: 0.38
  decompose_threshold: 0.75
  compress_threshold: 6000
  cache_ttl_seconds: 300

token_estimation:
  prose_chars_per_token: 3.8
  code_chars_per_token: 2.5
  overhead_factor: 1.2

docs.title: Adding a New Provider
docs.text:
1. Add one or more model entries to config/models.yaml with your provider's name:
- id: gpt-4o
  display_name: "GPT-4o"
  provider: openai
  model_string: "gpt-4o"
  context_tokens: 128000
  cost_input_per_1k: 0.005
  cost_output_per_1k: 0.015
  capability_rank: 9
  max_output_tokens: 16384
  strengths: [coding, reasoning, vision]
  1. Create model_router/providers/openai.py and subclass BaseProvider:
from model_router.providers.base import BaseProvider
from model_router.models import ExecutionRecord, TaskRequest

class OpenAIProvider(BaseProvider):
    provider_name = "openai"

    def supports_model(self, model_id: str) -> bool:
        return model_id.startswith("gpt-")

    async def execute(self, request, model_string, fallback_model_string=None):
        # call openai SDK here
        ...
        return result_text, record
  1. Register in model_router/router.py:
from model_router.providers.openai import OpenAIProvider

self._providers["openai"] = OpenAIProvider()
  1. Reload the config (dashboard → Settings → Reload, or router.reload_config()).

docs.title: CLI Options
docs.text: Subcommand Flag Default Description serve --host 127.0.0.1 Bind address for the dashboard serve --port 8765 Port for the dashboard run / preview prompt (stdin) Prompt text (positional or piped via stdin) run / preview --task-type (auto) One of coding, review, docs, reasoning, chat run / preview --quality 0.5 Quality requirement 0.0–1.0 run / preview --budget (none) Hard cost ceiling in USD

docs.title: Documentation System
docs.text: This project's documentation is automatically generated by doc-assistant — a Claude-powered documentation tool that runs on every git push.

docs.title: What runs on every push
docs.text:
Task What it produces Technical docs docs/ — architecture, API reference, configuration User guides docs/guides/ — getting started, usage, deployment Changelog CHANGELOG.md — version history from git commits HTML site site/index.html — this navigable site

All tasks run in the background so your push completes immediately. Progress is logged to .doc-assistant.log at the project root.

docs.title: Running manually
docs.text:
# From the project root
python ~/Projects/doc-assistant/main.py docs      .
python ~/Projects/doc-assistant/main.py guide     .
python ~/Projects/doc-assistant/main.py changelog .

# Rebuild the HTML site
python ~/Projects/doc-assistant/build_docs.py .

docs.title: Adding screenshots
docs.text:
Place screenshots in docs/assets/screenshots/ and embed them in any page:

![Login screen](../assets/screenshots/login.png)

See docs/assets/screenshots/README.md for naming conventions and format guidelines.

docs.title: Customising the documentation
docs.text: File Purpose mkdocs.yml Site structure, theme, navigation ~/Projects/doc-assistant/prompts/docs.md System prompt for technical docs ~/Projects/doc-assistant/prompts/guides.md System prompt for user guides ~/Projects/doc-assistant/prompts/changelog.md System prompt for changelog

docs.title: Viewing the site locally
docs.text:
# Serve with live reload (from project root)
mkdocs serve

# Or just open the built output directly
start site/index.html

docs.title: Source
docs.text: doc-assistant lives at ~/Projects/doc-assistant/. Its own documentation is at ~/Projects/doc-assistant/site/index.html.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
This page documents every public class, method, and data structure exported by the model_router package.

from model_router import (
    ModelRouter,
    TaskRequest,
    TaskType,
    RoutedResult,
    RoutingDecision,
    TaskFeatures,
    ExecutionRecord,
)

docs.title: ModelRouter
docs.text:
Module: model_router.router

Central orchestrator. Routes any TaskRequest to the best model, executes it, tracks usage, and returns a RoutedResult.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
ModelRouter(
    config_path: Path | None = None,
    tracker: UsageTracker | None = None,
)
Parameter Type Default Description config_path Path \| None config/models.yaml Path to the YAML configuration file tracker UsageTracker \| None new UsageTracker() Inject a custom tracker (useful for testing)

docs.title: route_task
docs.text:
async def route_task(request: TaskRequest) -> RoutingDecision

Analyse the request and return a RoutingDecision without executing any API call. Use this to preview which model would be selected.

decision = await router.route_task(TaskRequest(
    prompt="Design a distributed caching layer",
    quality_requirement=0.8,
))
print(decision.selected_model)   # "claude-opus-4-6"
print(decision.complexity_score) # 0.7821
print(decision.reasoning)        # human-readable explanation

docs.title: route_and_run
docs.text:
async def route_and_run(request: TaskRequest) -> RoutedResult

Full pipeline: compress → score → decompose? → execute → track → return.

Returns a RoutedResult with the generated content and full cost/token accounting.

result = await router.route_and_run(TaskRequest(
    prompt="Write a Python function to parse ISO 8601 dates",
    task_type=TaskType.CODING,
    quality_requirement=0.5,
    cost_budget_usd=0.01,
))
print(result.result)                 # generated text
print(result.decision.selected_model)
print(f"${result.total_cost_usd:.6f}")

Alias: execute is an alias for route_and_run available in older call sites.

docs.title: reload_config
docs.text:
def reload_config() -> None

Hot-reload models.yaml without restarting the process. Picks up changes to model definitions and routing thresholds immediately.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description session_id str UUID identifying the current router session registry ModelRegistry Access to the loaded model configuration

docs.title: TaskRequest
docs.text:
Module: model_router.models

Input to ModelRouter. All fields except prompt are optional.

@dataclass
class TaskRequest:
    prompt: str
    task_type: TaskType | None = None
    quality_requirement: float = 0.5
    cost_budget_usd: float | None = None
    cwd: str | None = None
    allowed_tools: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    system_prompt: str | None = None
    max_turns: int | None = None
    metadata: dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
    request_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
Field Type Default Description prompt str required The task description or user message task_type TaskType \| None None Explicit task type; auto-detected from keywords if omitted quality_requirement float 0.5 0.0 = cheapest/fastest, 1.0 = highest quality cost_budget_usd float \| None None Hard cost ceiling in USD; overrides threshold routing cwd str \| None None Working directory passed to claude-agent-sdk allowed_tools list[str] [] Tool names permitted for this task (disables caching when non-empty) system_prompt str \| None None Override system prompt for the SDK call max_turns int \| None None Maximum agentic turns; defaults to 30 inside the provider metadata dict {} Arbitrary caller metadata, not used by the router request_id str auto UUID Unique request identifier

docs.title: TaskType
docs.text:
Module: model_router.models

class TaskType(str, Enum):
    CODING    = "coding"
    REVIEW    = "review"
    DOCS      = "docs"
    REASONING = "reasoning"
    CHAT      = "chat"

Auto-detection uses keyword pattern matching. Provide an explicit value to override detection.

docs.title: RoutedResult
docs.text:
Module: model_router.models

Return value from route_and_run.

@dataclass
class RoutedResult:
    decision: RoutingDecision
    result: str | None
    records: list[ExecutionRecord]
    total_cost_usd: float
    total_input_tokens: int
    total_output_tokens: int
    duration_ms: int
    combined: bool = False
Field Type Description decision RoutingDecision The model-selection decision result str \| None Generated text; None on total failure records list[ExecutionRecord] One record per API call (multiple for decomposed tasks) total_cost_usd float Total cost across all subtasks total_input_tokens int Total input tokens consumed total_output_tokens int Total output tokens generated duration_ms int Wall-clock milliseconds from request to return combined bool True when subtask results were merged

docs.title: RoutingDecision
docs.text:
Module: model_router.models

The scorer's verdict — which model to use and why.

@dataclass
class RoutingDecision:
    selected_model: str
    selected_model_string: str
    fallback_model: str | None
    fallback_model_string: str | None
    complexity_score: float
    estimated_tokens: int
    task_type: TaskType
    decomposed: bool
    subtask_count: int
    reasoning: str
Field Type Description selected_model str Model ID from models.yaml (e.g. claude-opus-4-6) selected_model_string str Exact SDK model string fallback_model str \| None One-tier-down fallback model ID fallback_model_string str \| None Fallback SDK string complexity_score float Composite score 0.0–1.0 estimated_tokens int Pre-call token estimate task_type TaskType Detected or supplied task type decomposed bool True if the task will be split into subtasks subtask_count int Number of subtasks (0 if not decomposed) reasoning str Human-readable explanation of the decision

docs.title: TaskFeatures
docs.text:
Module: model_router.models

Intermediate feature vector produced by ComplexityScorer.extract_features(). Useful for debugging or custom scoring.

@dataclass
class TaskFeatures:
    char_count: int
    estimated_tokens: int
    keyword_complexity: float
    task_type: TaskType
    has_code: bool
    has_multifile: bool
    has_architecture: bool
    quality_requirement: float
    cost_budget_usd: float | None

docs.title: ExecutionRecord
docs.text:
Module: model_router.models

One row in data/usage.db. Returned inside RoutedResult.records.

@dataclass
class ExecutionRecord:
    record_id: str
    session_id: str
    model: str
    task_type: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cost_usd: float
    latency_ms: int
    timestamp: float
    complexity_score: float
    decomposed: bool
    subtask_index: int   # -1 if not decomposed
    success: bool
    error: str | None

docs.title: ComplexityScorer
docs.text:
Module: model_router.scorer

Analyses a TaskRequest and selects a model. Used internally by ModelRouter but can be instantiated independently.

scorer = ComplexityScorer(registry)

docs.title: extract_features
docs.text:
def extract_features(request: TaskRequest) -> TaskFeatures

Extract the five scoring signals from a request.

docs.title: composite_score
docs.text:
def composite_score(features: TaskFeatures) -> float

Compute the weighted composite complexity score (0.0–1.0).

docs.title: select_model
docs.text:
def select_model(features: TaskFeatures) -> str

Return the model ID best suited to the task, respecting any cost budget.

docs.title: build_routing_decision
docs.text:
def build_routing_decision(request: TaskRequest) -> tuple[TaskFeatures, RoutingDecision]

Convenience method that calls extract_features, composite_score, select_model, and assembles a full RoutingDecision.

docs.title: TaskDecomposer
docs.text:
Module: model_router.decomposer

Splits large tasks into subtasks. Used internally by ModelRouter.

docs.title: should_decompose
docs.text:
def should_decompose(features: TaskFeatures, model: ModelCapabilities) -> bool

Returns True when estimated tokens exceed decompose_threshold × context_tokens.

docs.title: estimate_subtask_count
docs.text:
def estimate_subtask_count(features: TaskFeatures, model: ModelCapabilities) -> int

Returns an integer between 2 and 8.

docs.title: decompose
docs.text:
def decompose(request: TaskRequest, features: TaskFeatures) -> list[Subtask]

Choose a decomposition strategy based on task type and return a list of Subtask objects.

docs.title: combine
docs.text:
def combine(subtask_results: list[str], strategy: str = "sequential") -> str

Merge subtask outputs. The sequential strategy concatenates results under ## Part N headers.

docs.title: TokenOptimizer
docs.text:
Module: model_router.optimizer

Reduces costs through prompt compression and result caching.

docs.title: compress_prompt
docs.text:
def compress_prompt(prompt: str, target_tokens: int) -> str

Apply up to three compression passes to bring the prompt under target_tokens. Returns the original string if no compression is needed.

docs.title: trim_context
docs.text:
def trim_context(
    messages: list[dict],
    max_tokens: int,
    chars_per_token: float = 4.0,
) -> list[dict]

Sliding-window trim of a message history list. Always preserves the system message, first user message, and the last three exchanges.

docs.title: build_cache_key
docs.text:
def build_cache_key(prompt: str, model: str) -> str

Return a SHA-256 hex string keyed on the normalised prompt and model name.

docs.title: get_cached / set_cached
docs.text:
def get_cached(key: str) -> str | None
def set_cached(key: str, result: str, ttl_seconds: int | None = None) -> None

Read from / write to the in-memory TTL cache.

docs.title: clear_expired
docs.text:
def clear_expired() -> int

Remove all expired entries and return the count of entries removed.

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description cache_size int Number of live (non-expired) entries in the cache

docs.title: ModelRegistry
docs.text:
Module: model_router.registry

Loads and queries config/models.yaml.

docs.title: get
docs.text:
def get(model_id: str) -> ModelCapabilities

Raises KeyError if the model ID is not found.

docs.title: all_models
docs.text:
def all_models() -> list[ModelCapabilities]

Return all models sorted by capability_rank descending.

docs.title: models_by_provider
docs.text: def models_by_provider(provider: str) -> list[ModelCapabilities]

docs.title: models_within_budget
docs.text:
def models_within_budget(budget_usd: float, estimated_tokens: int) -> list[ModelCapabilities]

Return models whose estimated cost (input + output/2) stays under budget_usd.

docs.title: estimate_cost
docs.text:
def estimate_cost(model_id: str, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float

Return estimated cost in USD.

docs.title: reload
docs.text:
def reload() -> None

Re-read models.yaml from disk. Thread-safe for single-process use.

docs.title: UsageTracker
docs.text:
Module: model_router.tracker

Persists execution records to SQLite and answers analytics queries.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text: UsageTracker(db_path: Path = Path("data/usage.db"))

docs.title: record
docs.text:
async def record(rec: ExecutionRecord) -> None

Write one record. Uses asyncio.to_thread so it never blocks the event loop.

docs.title: get_live_totals
docs.text:
async def get_live_totals() -> dict

Returns today's aggregated stats and per-model call counts:

{
    "today_cost_usd": 0.0042,
    "today_tokens": 15200,
    "today_calls": 12,
    "session_cost_usd": 0.0011,
    "session_tokens": 4100,
    "calls_per_model": {
        "claude-sonnet-4-6": {"calls": 8, "cost": 0.0008},
        ...
    }
}

docs.title: get_cost_by_model
docs.text:
async def get_cost_by_model(since_ts: float | None = None) -> list[dict]

Aggregate stats grouped by model since the given Unix timestamp (or all time).

docs.title: get_task_history
docs.text:
async def get_task_history(limit: int = 100, offset: int = 0) -> list[dict]

Return paginated raw records ordered by timestamp descending.

docs.title: get_session_stats
docs.text:
async def get_session_stats() -> dict

Return summary stats for the current session only.

docs.title: BaseProvider
docs.text:
Module: model_router.providers.base

Abstract base class for all AI provider adapters.

class BaseProvider(ABC):
    provider_name: str = ""

    @abstractmethod
    async def execute(
        self,
        request: TaskRequest,
        model_string: str,
        fallback_model_string: str | None = None,
    ) -> tuple[str | None, ExecutionRecord]: ...

    @abstractmethod
    def supports_model(self, model_id: str) -> bool: ...

Implement both methods and register your provider in ModelRouter.__init__:

self._providers["myprovider"] = MyProvider()

docs.title: REST API
docs.text:
The dashboard server exposes these endpoints on http://localhost:8765 (default).

Method Path Description GET /api/health Health check; returns version and uptime GET /api/stats/live SSE stream; emits live totals every 2 seconds GET /api/stats/session Session-scoped aggregate stats GET /api/stats/cost-by-model Cost and call count grouped by model (?since=<unix_ts>) GET /api/history Paginated task history (?limit=&offset=) GET /api/models List all registered models POST /api/models/reload Hot-reload config/models.yaml POST /api/route Preview routing decision for a prompt (no execution)

docs.title: POST /api/route — request body
docs.text:
{
  "prompt": "Refactor the auth module to use JWT",
  "task_type": "coding",
  "quality_requirement": 0.7,
  "cost_budget_usd": null
}

docs.title: POST /api/route — response
docs.text:
{
  "selected_model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "selected_model_string": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "complexity_score": 0.4812,
  "estimated_tokens": 320,
  "task_type": "coding",
  "decomposed": false,
  "subtask_count": 0,
  "reasoning": "Complexity score: 0.48. Task type: coding. Estimated tokens: 320. Code content detected. Selected: Sonnet 4.6 (balanced)."
}

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text:
Place application screenshots in this folder and reference them in the documentation using:

![Description](../assets/screenshots/your-screenshot.png)

docs.title: Naming conventions
docs.text: Screen Suggested filename Main / home screen home.png Login / authentication login.png Dashboard dashboard.png Settings settings.png Feature-specific feature-<name>.png

docs.title: Recommended format
docs.text:
• Format: PNG (or WebP for smaller files)
  • Resolution: At least 1280 x 720 px
  • Annotations: Use a tool like Snagit, ShareX, or Greenshot to add callouts

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: By the end of this guide you will have Model Router installed, configured, and running your first routed task — both from the command line and from Python code. The whole process takes about five minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Requirement Version Notes Python ≥ 3.11 Check with python --version pip latest recommended Comes with Python Anthropic API key — Create one at console.anthropic.com

Use a virtual environment

Model Router installs several dependencies. A virtual environment keeps them isolated from your system Python.

docs.title: 1 — Clone and install
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository and enter the project directory:

    git clone <repo-url>
cd model-router

  2. (Recommended) Create and activate a virtual environment:

    macOS / LinuxWindows 
    python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

    python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\activate

  3. Install all dependencies from the lock file:

    pip install -r requirements.txt

    Expected outcome — pip installs claude-agent-sdk, pydantic, PyYAML, starlette, uvicorn, sse-starlette, httpx, and anyio without errors.

  4. Verify the CLI is available:

    python main.py --help

    You should see the serve, run, and preview subcommands listed.

docs.title: 2 — Set your API key
docs.text:
Model Router calls the Claude API through the claude-agent-sdk. The SDK reads the key from an environment variable.

macOS / LinuxWindows (PowerShell)Windows (Command Prompt) 

export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."
Add this line to your ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc to make it permanent.

$env:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = "sk-ant-..."

set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

Keep your key secret

Never commit your API key to version control. If you use a .env file, add it to .gitignore.

docs.title: 3 — Preview your first routing decision
docs.text:
The preview subcommand scores a prompt and shows which model would be chosen — without making an API call or spending any money. It is the fastest way to understand how the router thinks.

python main.py preview "Refactor this microservice to use async database queries"

Expected output:

{
  "selected_model": "claude-opus-4-6",
  "selected_model_string": "claude-opus-4-6",
  "complexity_score": 0.7821,
  "task_type": "coding",
  "estimated_tokens": 840,
  "decomposed": false,
  "subtask_count": 0,
  "reasoning": "Complexity score: 0.78. Task type: coding. Estimated tokens: 840. Architecture/design signals detected. Code content detected. Selected: Opus 4.6 (highest capability)."
}

The five-signal scoring formula is:

score = 0.35 × token_score
      + 0.30 × keyword_complexity
      + 0.20 × quality_requirement
      + 0.10 × architecture_signal
      + 0.05 × multifile_signal
Score range Model selected ≥ 0.72 Claude Opus 4.6 — highest capability 0.38 – 0.72 Claude Sonnet 4.6 — balanced speed and quality < 0.38 Claude Haiku 4.5 — fastest and cheapest

Try a simpler prompt and notice the model changes:

python main.py preview "What is the capital of France?"

docs.title: 4 — Run your first task
docs.text:
The run subcommand executes the routed task and prints the result.

python main.py run "Explain the difference between TCP and UDP in one paragraph" --quality 0.3

Expected output:

Model  : claude-haiku-4-5
Tokens : 312
Cost   : $0.00008
Latency: 843ms

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) guarantees delivery by establishing a
connection, ordering packets, and retransmitting lost data — at the cost of
extra overhead. UDP (User Datagram Protocol) sends packets without a
handshake, making it faster but unreliable, which suits real-time applications
like video calls or gaming where occasional packet loss is acceptable.

Every call is automatically recorded to data/usage.db (SQLite). You can query it at any time without touching the running server.

docs.title: Useful flags
docs.text:
Flag Description Example --quality 0.0–1.0 0 = cheapest/fastest, 1 = best quality --quality 0.9 --task-type Skip auto-detection --task-type coding --budget Hard USD ceiling; selects the best model that fits --budget 0.01

Force a specific task type:

python main.py run --task-type coding \
  "Write a Python dataclass for a paginated API response"

Cap spend at one cent:

python main.py run --budget 0.01 \
  "Summarise the main ideas of the CAP theorem"

Pipe a long prompt from a file:

cat my_prompt.txt | python main.py run --task-type review

docs.title: 5 — Use the Python API
docs.text:
Import ModelRouter directly when you want to embed routing in your own application.

import anyio
from model_router import ModelRouter, TaskRequest, TaskType

# Instantiate once; reuse across requests
router = ModelRouter()

result = anyio.run(
    router.route_and_run,
    TaskRequest(
        prompt="Write unit tests for a binary search implementation",
        task_type=TaskType.CODING,   # optional — auto-detected if omitted
        quality_requirement=0.6,     # 0.0 (cheap) → 1.0 (best)
    ),
)

print(result.result)                                          # generated text
print(f"Model  : {result.decision.selected_model}")          # e.g. claude-sonnet-4-6
print(f"Tokens : {result.total_input_tokens + result.total_output_tokens}")
print(f"Cost   : ${result.total_cost_usd:.6f}")
print(f"Time   : {result.duration_ms}ms")

Preview without executing (Python):

import anyio
from model_router import ModelRouter, TaskRequest

router = ModelRouter()
decision = anyio.run(
    router.route_task,
    TaskRequest(prompt="Design a distributed event-sourcing system"),
)

print(decision.selected_model)    # e.g. claude-opus-4-6
print(decision.complexity_score)  # e.g. 0.85
print(decision.reasoning)

Inside an async application

If you are already in an async context (FastAPI, Starlette, etc.), call await router.route_and_run(request) directly — no need for anyio.run().

docs.title: 6 — Launch the live dashboard
docs.text:
The dashboard gives you real-time cost and usage data, plus an interactive Route Preview tool.

python main.py serve

Open http://localhost:8765 in your browser.

📷 Screenshot: Dashboard homepage showing stat cards (Today's Cost, Tokens, Calls, Session Cost) and a model usage bar chart

Custom host and port:

python main.py serve --host 0.0.0.0 --port 9000

Remote access

Binding to 0.0.0.0 exposes the dashboard on all network interfaces. Only do this on trusted networks or behind a reverse proxy.

docs.title: Route Preview tool
docs.text:
Navigate to Tools → Route Preview in the sidebar. Paste any prompt, adjust the quality slider, and click Analyse Task to see the routing decision and full reasoning — no API call is made.

📷 Screenshot: Route Preview page with a prompt entered in the left panel and the routing decision JSON displayed in the right panel

docs.title: 7 — Understand the default configuration
docs.text:
All routing thresholds live in config/models.yaml. No changes are needed to get started, but it is worth knowing what the defaults are:

routing:
  opus_threshold: 0.72      # complexity >= this -> Opus
  sonnet_threshold: 0.38    # complexity >= this -> Sonnet, else Haiku
  compress_threshold: 6000  # compress prompt when estimated tokens exceed this
  cache_ttl_seconds: 300    # in-memory result cache TTL (5 minutes)

Changes to models.yaml take effect immediately without restarting the server — click Settings → Reload in the dashboard or call router.reload_config() in Python.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Guide What you will learn Usage All CLI flags, Python API patterns, querying usage data, hot-reloading config Configuration Every option in models.yaml, routing thresholds, token estimation, adding providers Troubleshooting Common errors and how to fix them Architecture How the scoring, decomposition, and caching pipeline works internally API Reference Every public class, method, and data structure

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: This page lists the most common problems encountered when installing or using Model Router, along with step-by-step fixes.

docs.title: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'model_router'
docs.text:
Cause: Python cannot find the project package — most often because the virtual environment is not activated, or pip install was run in the wrong environment.

Fix:

  1. Make sure you are in the project root (the directory that contains main.py):

    ls main.py          # should print "main.py"

  2. Activate the virtual environment:

    macOS / LinuxWindows 
    source .venv/bin/activate

    .venv\Scripts\activate

  3. Re-run the install:

    pip install -r requirements.txt

  4. Run from the project root (not from inside model_router/):

    python main.py --help

docs.title: pip install fails with dependency errors
docs.text:
Cause: An outdated pip or a conflicting package version.

Fix:

  1. Upgrade pip first:

    python -m pip install --upgrade pip

  2. Retry the install:

    pip install -r requirements.txt

If a specific package still fails, install it individually to see the full error:

pip install claude-agent-sdk
pip install "pydantic>=2.0"

docs.title: Python version is older than 3.11
docs.text:
Symptom: Syntax errors on startup, or pip rejects the package.

Fix: Install Python 3.11 or newer from python.org and create a fresh virtual environment:

python3.11 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate    # macOS / Linux
pip install -r requirements.txt

Managing multiple Python versions

Tools like pyenv (macOS/Linux) or py launcher (Windows) make it easy to switch between Python versions without affecting your system installation.

docs.title: AuthenticationError / 401 Unauthorized
docs.text:
Cause: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set, is set incorrectly, or the key has been revoked.

Fix:

  1. Verify the variable is exported in the current shell:

    macOS / LinuxWindows (PowerShell) 
    echo $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

    echo $env:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

    The output should start with sk-ant-. If it is blank, set it:

    export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

  2. Check that your key is active in the Anthropic Console.

  3. If you set the key in a new terminal tab, re-activate the virtual environment — environment variables are not inherited across some shell configurations.

docs.title: Key is set but still getting auth errors
docs.text:
Cause: The key may have leading/trailing spaces, or a shell alias is overriding python.

Fix:

# Unset and re-set to remove accidental whitespace
unset ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

# Confirm the key value looks right
python -c "import os; k = os.getenv('ANTHROPIC_API_KEY',''); print(repr(k[:15]))"

docs.title: Every prompt routes to Opus regardless of content
docs.text:
Cause: quality_requirement defaults to 0.5; prompts with architecture or code keywords score high enough to hit the opus_threshold (0.72). Or the threshold has been lowered in config/models.yaml.

Fix — lower the quality requirement for simple tasks:

python main.py run "What year was Python created?" --quality 0.1

Fix — raise the Opus threshold so fewer tasks reach it:

# config/models.yaml
routing:
  opus_threshold: 0.82    # was 0.72

Then reload: router.reload_config() or dashboard → Settings → Reload.

docs.title: Task is unexpectedly decomposed into subtasks
docs.text:
Cause: The estimated token count exceeds decompose_threshold × context_window. This happens with very long prompts.

Fix:

  • Shorten the prompt.

  • Or raise decompose_threshold in config/models.yaml (max 1.0 disables decomposition):

    routing:
  decompose_threshold: 1.0    # never decompose

docs.title: ValueError: No provider registered for '...'
docs.text:
Cause: A model entry in config/models.yaml references a provider key (e.g. openai) that has not been registered in model_router/router.py.

Fix:

  1. Check your models.yaml for any model with an unrecognised provider: field.
  2. Either remove or comment out that model entry, or follow the Adding a Provider instructions to implement and register the provider.

docs.title: result.result is None
docs.text:
Cause: The provider's execute() call returned no content — most often due to an API error that was silently caught, or a max_turns limit that was reached before any output was produced.

Fix:

  1. Check result.records for any record where success == False or error is set:

    for rec in result.records:
    if not rec.success:
        print(rec.error)

  2. Increase max_turns in TaskRequest if you are using tool-using agents.

  3. Retry with a shorter prompt (token limits may be exceeded for very large inputs).

docs.title: Dashboard won't start: Address already in use
docs.text:
Cause: Port 8765 is already taken — either by another Model Router instance or another application.

Fix:

# Use a different port
python main.py serve --port 9001

Or find and kill the process using port 8765:

macOS / LinuxWindows (PowerShell) 
lsof -ti:8765 | xargs kill

Get-Process -Id (Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 8765).OwningProcess | Stop-Process

docs.title: Dashboard loads but shows no data
docs.text:
Cause: The dashboard was opened in the browser before any run commands were executed in the same or a previous session.

Fix: Run at least one task and refresh the dashboard:

python main.py run "Hello" --quality 0.1

If data still does not appear, check that data/usage.db exists and is not empty:

sqlite3 data/usage.db "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM api_calls;"

docs.title: SSE live stats are not updating
docs.text:
Cause: The browser or a proxy is buffering the server-sent events stream.

Fix:

  1. Try opening the dashboard in a different browser.

  2. If you are behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy), add these headers:

    proxy_set_header Cache-Control no-cache;
proxy_buffering off;
proxy_read_timeout 86400s;

docs.title: Config changes are not taking effect
docs.text:
Cause: models.yaml was edited but the reload was not triggered.

Fix — programmatic:

router.reload_config()

Fix — dashboard:

Navigate to Settings → Reload and confirm the success message.

Fix — restart:

Stop the server (Ctrl+C) and start it again:

python main.py serve

docs.title: yaml.scanner.ScannerError on startup
docs.text:
Cause: Invalid YAML syntax in config/models.yaml — typically a missing colon, wrong indentation, or an unquoted special character.

Fix:

  1. Validate the file with a YAML linter:

    python -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('config/models.yaml'))"

  2. Fix the reported line.

  3. Common pitfalls in YAML:

    # Wrong — unquoted colon in value
display_name: Claude: Opus 4.6

# Right
display_name: "Claude: Opus 4.6"

docs.title: Costs are higher than expected
docs.text:
Tip 1 — lower the default quality:

python main.py run "..." --quality 0.3

Tip 2 — set a hard budget ceiling:

python main.py run "..." --budget 0.005

Tip 3 — enable prompt compression by lowering compress_threshold:

routing:
  compress_threshold: 2000   # compress any prompt over ~2 000 tokens

Tip 4 — check the cache TTL. If similar prompts are repeated within 5 minutes they should be served from cache at zero cost. Verify cache_ttl_seconds is not set to 0.

docs.title: Response latency is very high
docs.text:
Cause: Large prompts, many subtasks, or slow network.

Tips:

  • Use --quality 0.1 to route to Haiku, which is 3–5× faster than Opus.
  • Check decision.decomposed — decomposed tasks run subtasks sequentially. If decomposition is not needed, raise decompose_threshold to prevent it.
  • Use preview first to estimate token count and confirm you are not accidentally sending a huge prompt.

docs.title: Still stuck?
docs.text:
1. Run python main.py --help to confirm the CLI is installed correctly.

  2. Check data/usage.db for failed records:

    SELECT * FROM api_calls WHERE success = 0 ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT 10;

  3. Review the Configuration reference to confirm models.yaml is valid.

  4. Open an issue and include the output of:

    python main.py preview "test"
python --version
pip show claude-agent-sdk pydantic starlette

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: This guide covers day-to-day use of Model Router: running tasks from the CLI, embedding it in Python applications, controlling cost and quality, and querying the usage database.

docs.title: preview — inspect routing without executing
docs.text:
Use preview whenever you want to understand why a prompt would be sent to a particular model without spending any API credits.

python main.py preview "<prompt>" [flags]
Flag Default Description --task-type auto-detected One of coding, review, docs, reasoning, chat --quality 0.5 Quality requirement 0.0–1.0 (higher = more likely to pick Opus) --budget none Hard USD ceiling (changes model selection logic)

Examples:

# Auto-detect task type
python main.py preview "Implement a thread-safe LRU cache in Python"

# Override quality to see how it affects model selection
python main.py preview "Write a haiku" --quality 0.1
python main.py preview "Write a haiku" --quality 0.95

# Preview with a budget constraint
python main.py preview "Analyse this codebase for security vulnerabilities" --budget 0.005

The output is a JSON object you can pipe to jq or parse in scripts:

python main.py preview "Explain CRDT data structures" | jq .selected_model

docs.title: run — execute a routed task
docs.text:
python main.py run "<prompt>" [flags]

Same flags as preview. The command prints the model used, token count, cost, latency, and then the full response.

Inline prompt:

python main.py run "List five common HTTP caching headers and their purpose" --quality 0.4

Piped prompt from stdin:

echo "Summarise this article:" | cat - article.txt | python main.py run --task-type docs

Task types and when to use them:

Task type Auto-detected when prompt contains… Best model tier coding function, class, implement, refactor, bug, test, async Sonnet / Opus review review, audit, check, security, performance, improve Sonnet / Opus docs document, readme, guide, explain, describe, comment Haiku / Sonnet reasoning analyse, design, architecture, tradeoff, why, evaluate Opus chat general conversation, simple questions Haiku

When to override --task-type

Auto-detection works well for most prompts. Override it when your prompt is brief and context-free — for example, a short prompt piped from a file that lacks the keywords the scorer looks for.

docs.title: serve — start the monitoring dashboard
docs.text:
python main.py serve [--host HOST] [--port PORT]
Flag Default Description --host 127.0.0.1 Bind address (use 0.0.0.0 to expose on all interfaces) --port 8765 TCP port

The dashboard provides:

  • Live stat cards — Today's cost, token count, call count, session cost (updated via SSE)
  • Model usage chart — calls and cost breakdown by model
  • Task history — every recorded call with model, tokens, cost, latency
  • Route Preview — interactive tool to score any prompt without executing it
📷 Screenshot: Dashboard with the Task History table open, showing several rows with model, task type, tokens, cost, and latency columns

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
import anyio
from model_router import ModelRouter, TaskRequest, TaskType

router = ModelRouter()   # reads config/models.yaml automatically

result = anyio.run(
    router.route_and_run,
    TaskRequest(prompt="Explain the actor model of concurrency"),
)

print(result.result)

docs.title: All TaskRequest fields
docs.text:
from model_router import TaskRequest, TaskType

request = TaskRequest(
    # Required
    prompt="Refactor the payment service to use async I/O",

    # Optional — all have sensible defaults
    task_type=TaskType.CODING,      # coding | review | docs | reasoning | chat
                                    # auto-detected from prompt if omitted
    quality_requirement=0.8,        # 0.0 (cheapest) → 1.0 (best); default 0.5
    cost_budget_usd=0.02,           # hard ceiling in USD; None = unlimited
    system_prompt="You are a senior Python engineer.",
    max_turns=3,                    # max agent turns (passed to claude-agent-sdk)
    allowed_tools=["read_file"],    # tools forwarded to the SDK
    cwd="/path/to/project",        # working directory for tool-using agents
    metadata={"user_id": "u123"},   # arbitrary key-value pairs for your records
)

docs.title: Async usage (FastAPI / Starlette)
docs.text:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from model_router import ModelRouter, TaskRequest

app = FastAPI()
router = ModelRouter()   # create once at startup

@app.post("/ask")
async def ask(body: dict):
    result = await router.route_and_run(TaskRequest(prompt=body["prompt"]))
    return {
        "answer": result.result,
        "model": result.decision.selected_model,
        "cost_usd": result.total_cost_usd,
    }

docs.title: Preview routing decision in Python
docs.text:
import anyio
from model_router import ModelRouter, TaskRequest

router = ModelRouter()

decision = anyio.run(
    router.route_task,
    TaskRequest(
        prompt="Implement a distributed rate limiter using Redis",
        quality_requirement=0.9,
    ),
)

print(f"Model          : {decision.selected_model}")
print(f"Complexity     : {decision.complexity_score:.4f}")
print(f"Task type      : {decision.task_type}")
print(f"Est. tokens    : {decision.estimated_tokens}")
print(f"Will decompose : {decision.decomposed} ({decision.subtask_count} subtasks)")
print(f"Reasoning      : {decision.reasoning}")

docs.title: Inspecting RoutedResult
docs.text:
result = anyio.run(router.route_and_run, request)

# The generated response
print(result.result)

# Routing metadata
print(result.decision.selected_model)
print(result.decision.complexity_score)
print(result.decision.decomposed)        # True if task was split into subtasks

# Cost and token totals across all subtasks
print(result.total_cost_usd)
print(result.total_input_tokens)
print(result.total_output_tokens)
print(result.duration_ms)

# Individual execution records (one per subtask if decomposed, else one)
for rec in result.records:
    print(rec.model, rec.input_tokens, rec.output_tokens, rec.cost_usd)

docs.title: The quality_requirement dial
docs.text:
quality_requirement (0.0–1.0) feeds directly into the complexity score:

Value Practical effect 0.0 – 0.2 Strongly biases towards Haiku; choose for bulk/cheap tasks 0.3 – 0.6 Default range; model is chosen primarily by prompt complexity 0.7 – 0.9 Biases towards Sonnet and Opus 1.0 Maximum quality signal; Opus will be chosen for almost any prompt 
# Fast and cheap — simple summary
python main.py run "Summarise this paragraph in one sentence" --quality 0.1

# High-quality — complex architecture question
python main.py run "Design a CQRS + event-sourcing system for an e-commerce platform" --quality 0.95

docs.title: The --budget / cost_budget_usd cap
docs.text:
When a budget is set, Model Router ignores the complexity thresholds and instead picks the highest-capability model whose estimated cost fits within the ceiling.

# Never spend more than half a cent on this call
python main.py run "Review this SQL query for performance issues" --budget 0.005

result = anyio.run(
    router.route_and_run,
    TaskRequest(
        prompt="Analyse this 10 000-line codebase for architectural issues",
        cost_budget_usd=0.05,
    ),
)

Budget vs quality

Setting both quality_requirement and cost_budget_usd is valid. The budget wins: routing will not exceed the ceiling even if the quality signal suggests Opus.

docs.title: Querying usage data
docs.text: Every executed task is persisted to data/usage.db (SQLite). You can query it directly.

docs.title: Open the database
docs.text: sqlite3 data/usage.db

docs.title: Useful queries
docs.text:
Total cost and tokens today:

SELECT
    ROUND(SUM(cost_usd), 6)              AS today_cost_usd,
    SUM(input_tokens + output_tokens)    AS today_tokens,
    COUNT(*)                             AS calls
FROM api_calls
WHERE timestamp >= strftime('%s', 'now', 'start of day');

Breakdown by model:

SELECT
    model,
    COUNT(*)                          AS calls,
    SUM(input_tokens)                 AS total_input,
    SUM(output_tokens)                AS total_output,
    ROUND(SUM(cost_usd), 6)          AS total_cost_usd,
    ROUND(AVG(latency_ms))           AS avg_latency_ms
FROM api_calls
GROUP BY model
ORDER BY total_cost_usd DESC;

Most expensive calls:

SELECT
    datetime(timestamp, 'unixepoch', 'localtime') AS time,
    model,
    task_type,
    input_tokens + output_tokens AS tokens,
    ROUND(cost_usd, 6)           AS cost_usd,
    latency_ms
FROM api_calls
ORDER BY cost_usd DESC
LIMIT 20;

Session summary:

SELECT
    session_id,
    COUNT(*)                 AS calls,
    ROUND(SUM(cost_usd), 6) AS session_cost,
    MIN(datetime(timestamp, 'unixepoch', 'localtime')) AS started
FROM api_calls
GROUP BY session_id
ORDER BY started DESC
LIMIT 10;

docs.title: Programmatic access
docs.text:
Use UsageTracker for async access in Python:

import anyio
from model_router.tracker import UsageTracker

tracker = UsageTracker()

# Today's totals
totals = anyio.run(tracker.get_live_totals)
print(totals["today_cost_usd"])
print(totals["calls_per_model"])

# Full cost breakdown by model
breakdown = anyio.run(tracker.get_cost_by_model)
for row in breakdown:
    print(row["model"], row["calls"], row["cost"])

# Recent task history
history = anyio.run(tracker.get_task_history, 50)   # last 50 calls
for row in history:
    print(row["model"], row["task_type"], row["cost_usd"])

docs.title: Hot-reloading configuration
docs.text:
You can change routing thresholds and add models while the server is running.

From the dashboard:

  1. Edit config/models.yaml
  2. Open http://localhost:8765
  3. Navigate to Settings → Reload

From Python:

router.reload_config()
print("Config reloaded — new thresholds are active immediately")

From the CLI (one-liner):

python - <<'EOF'
import anyio
from model_router.router import ModelRouter
r = ModelRouter()
r.reload_config()
print("Reloaded")
EOF

docs.title: Result caching
docs.text:
Model Router keeps an in-memory SHA-256-keyed cache of recent results. If you send the same prompt with the same task type within the TTL window (cache_ttl_seconds, default 300 s), the cached result is returned immediately at zero cost.

import anyio, time
from model_router import ModelRouter, TaskRequest

router = ModelRouter()
req = TaskRequest(prompt="What is a monad?")

# First call hits the API
r1 = anyio.run(router.route_and_run, req)
print(f"Cost: ${r1.total_cost_usd:.6f}, time: {r1.duration_ms}ms")

# Second call is served from cache (cost = $0.000000)
r2 = anyio.run(router.route_and_run, req)
print(f"Cost: ${r2.total_cost_usd:.6f}, time: {r2.duration_ms}ms")

Disabling the cache

Set cache_ttl_seconds: 0 in config/models.yaml to disable caching entirely, then call router.reload_config().
